title: The Sample Complexity of Smooth Boosting and the Tightness of the Hardcore Theorem
speaker: Guy Blanc
abstract:
This talk will be about two related results, one in complexity theory and one in learning.
On the learning side, we investigate the sample complexity of smooth boosters - These are boosting algorithms that do not place too much weight on any given example. They have a variety of applications including robustness, privacy, and reproducibility. We show that the sample complexity overhead of existing smooth boosters is optimal, separating smooth boosting from distribution-independent boosting.
On the complexity side, our work also sheds new light on Impagliazzo's hardcore theorem from complexity theory, all known proofs of which can be cast in the framework of smooth boosting. For a function f that is mildly hard against size-s circuits, the hardcore theorem provides a set of inputs on which f is extremely hard against size-s′ circuits. A downside of this important result is the loss in circuit size, i.e. that s′≪s. Answering a question of Trevisan, we show that this size loss is necessary and in fact, the parameters achieved by known proofs are the best possible.

proof_stages.label: Smooth boosting sample-complexity problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines weak and strong learning, contrasts ordinary boosting with smooth boosting, and states the first main result that smooth boosting can require essentially an m/gamma^2 sample overhead for strong learning even when weak learning over smooth distributions uses m samples.

proof_stages.label: Hardcore theorem and gamma-squared loss
proof_stages.summary: Hardness amplification and Impagliazzo's hardcore theorem are introduced, related to smooth boosting by a contrapositive argument, and the second main result is stated: the gamma^2 circuit-size loss in the hardcore theorem is unconditionally necessary.

proof_stages.label: Random majority-of-functions construction
proof_stages.summary: Using Shannon-style circuit-size bounds for random functions, the hard function is constructed as the majority of k independent random balanced n-bit functions, with the guiding intuition that weak approximation can compute one block while high accuracy requires many blocks.

proof_stages.label: Junta and hierarchical-circuit reduction
proof_stages.summary: The proof analyzes the majority function through junta complexity—one coordinate gives weak advantage on smooth distributions, but high constant accuracy needs linearly many coordinates—and translates this into upper and lower bounds for hierarchical circuits that compute selected inner blocks exactly.

proof_stages.label: Lifting from juntas to arbitrary circuits
proof_stages.summary: A lifting theorem is proved in the needed special case using soft juntas, correlation bounds for random balanced functions, hierarchically-valued circuits, and a triangle-inequality covering argument, thereby turning junta lower bounds into lower bounds against all small circuits.

proof_stages.label: Tightness of the hardcore theorem
proof_stages.summary: Applying the lifting theorem to the random majority construction yields a function that is weakly approximable on every large set by circuits of size about gamma^2 s but requires size s for 99% accuracy, proving tightness of the hardcore-theorem size loss.

proof_stages.label: Transfer to smooth boosting and efficient-boosting question
proof_stages.summary: The same majority-based concept class gives the smooth-boosting sample lower bound, with the hardcore lower-bound analysis transferring to sample complexity while the weak-learning upper bound incurs a logarithmic loss, and the talk closes with an open question about whether time-efficient standard boosting can achieve the optimal m/gamma sample overhead.

examples.name: One-percent adversarial sample corruption
examples.note: The corruption scenario shows why ordinary boosting can destroy robustness by upweighting corrupted points, motivating the move to smooth boosting.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces how 1% adversarial corruptions get upweighted during boosting and can become a much larger effective corruption

examples.name: Subsampling and reusing data in boosting
examples.note: An audience question proposes subsampling as a possible technique for the open efficient-boosting problem, and the speaker relates it to known PAC-learning sample-complexity tricks while noting that the needed analysis is unclear.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker discusses the subsampling procedure—drawing subsamples, running learners, combining by majority, and comparing possible m and gamma sample scales
